metadata.title: Odysseus AI: Self-Hosted AI Workspace Guide
metadata.description: Independent Odysseus AI guide with official links, setup boundaries, and local AI workspace comparison notes.

page.title: Odysseus AI: Self-Hosted AI Workspace Guide

hero.title: Check Odysseus AI before running a self-hosted workspace
hero.description: Odysseus is drawing attention as a self-hosted AI workspace. Use this independent guide to open the official project, read setup notes, and understand the practical checks before exposing local AI tools.
warnings: This page does not mirror downloads or replace the official repository.
Review authentication, ports, private data, and model/API configuration before running any local AI workspace.
Treat third-party installers and community guides as convenience layers, not as primary sources.

sources.title: Official Odysseus project page
sources.description: Start with the live project page before following any third-party setup instructions.

sources.title: Odysseus GitHub repository
sources.description: Read the source repository, issues, and current branch state before installing.

sources.title: Setup documentation
sources.description: Use the repository setup docs as the primary setup reference.

sources.title: Hacker News discussion
sources.description: Scan community questions and caveats before choosing a self-hosted workflow.

workflow.title: Odysseus AI validation checklist
workflow.description: Use this sequence before deciding whether Odysseus belongs in your local AI stack.

items.title: Confirm the source
items.description: Open the official site and repository first. Avoid copied installers, mirrored files, or pages that hide the original source.

items.title: Map the workspace role
items.description: Decide whether you need chat, local agents, model routing, research tools, mail, or a broader workspace before installing.

items.title: Check local exposure
items.description: Review ports, authentication, private data paths, and network access before running a self-hosted AI app.

items.title: Compare alternatives
items.description: If your goal is only local chat, RAG, or tool wiring, compare Odysseus with narrower options such as Open WebUI, AnythingLLM, Pinokio workflows, or an MCP-first editor setup.

comparison.title: When Odysseus AI may be worth testing
comparison.description: The strongest fit is a local-first AI workspace, not a one-click chatbot replacement.

items.title: You want a workspace, not only chat
items.description: Odysseus is interesting when your workflow needs multiple tools and local workspace structure around AI tasks.

items.title: You can inspect source and setup
items.description: Self-hosted AI tools should be reviewed like infrastructure. Read the repository and setup instructions before running them.

items.title: You need privacy-aware control
items.description: Local-first tools can reduce third-party exposure, but only if you configure models, storage, and network access carefully.

items.title: You are wiring local tools through MCP
items.description: Use the MCP config scratchpad when your local AI workspace depends on file, browser, database, or shell-connected MCP servers.

faq.title: Odysseus AI FAQ
faq.description: Independent notes for searchers comparing Odysseus with local AI workspace tools.

items.question: Is Motubrain.org the official Odysseus AI website?
items.answer: No. This is an independent guide. Use the official Odysseus project page and GitHub repository for primary information.

items.question: Does this page host an Odysseus download?
items.answer: No. Motubrain.org does not mirror downloads. The source links point to the official project and repository.

items.question: Is Odysseus AI the same as Open WebUI or AnythingLLM?
items.answer: No. They may overlap around local AI workflows, but each project has different scope, setup, and assumptions. Compare the official docs before choosing one.

items.question: What should I check before self-hosting an AI workspace?
items.answer: Check source authenticity, current setup docs, exposed ports, authentication, model/API configuration, storage paths, and whether private data leaves your machine.
